serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried figs is approximately 3 pieces (about 40 g), commonly consumed as a snack or added to cereals, salads, or baked goods.

nutrient_source.title: Dried fig — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for dried fig (F04030020). All per-100g values preserved from the input record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Figs, dried — Glycemic Index entry, University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 61 (medium) for dried figs sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 61 × 54.5 × 0.40 / 100 = 13.3 (serving size 40 g, available carbohydrate 21.8 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (52.6 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention; partially offset by high potassium (780 mg/100g) and calcium (200 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit dried fig intake to 1–2 pieces per occasion (~20–30 g) to minimise the sugar-driven insulin response that promotes sodium retention, while benefiting from the high potassium content only at controlled portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 52.6 g/100g far exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling; medium GI of 61 combined with concentrated sugar load causes significant postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit dried figs to no more than 1 small piece (~10 g) per occasion, paired with protein or healthy fat to blunt the glycaemic response, and should prefer fresh figs for a lower sugar concentration.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre (14.3 g/100g) and potassium (780 mg/100g) support cardiovascular health, but the very high sugar content (52.6 g/100g) at typical snack portions may contribute to hypertriglyceridaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, dried figs should be consumed in small portions (≤30 g per serving) to leverage the fibre and potassium benefits while avoiding the triglyceride-raising effect of excess fructose from the concentrated sugar load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar content of 52.6 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g RA threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit dried fig consumption to 1–2 pieces maximum per occasion and consider fresh figs as a lower-sugar, anti-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried fig (Ficus carica) is a minimally processed fruit produced by dehydrating fresh common figs, resulting in a dark brown, chewy product with a concentrated natural honey-like sweetness and a distinctive seedy crunch from the numerous small seeds inside. The drying process removes most of the water content, concentrating all nutrients and sugars dramatically compared to fresh figs. Per 100 g, dried figs provide 259 kcal, 3.6 g protein, 0.7 g fat, 54.5 g carbohydrate (of which 52.6 g is sugar), and an exceptionally high dietary fibre content of 14.3 g. They are also a notable source of potassium (780 mg/100g) and calcium (200 mg/100g), making them nutritionally dense. Dried figs have a medium glycemic index of approximately 61, meaning they cause a moderate rise in blood glucose, but the very high sugar concentration means even small portions deliver a substantial glycaemic load. Clinically, dried figs are unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or rheumatoid arthritis at typical snack portions due to the extreme sugar concentration. For hypertension and cardiovascular disease, the high potassium and fibre content offer benefit, but must be balanced against the sugar load. Dried figs are widely consumed as a snack, in baked goods, salads, cheese boards, and traditional Middle Eastern and Mediterranean cuisine. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal.
common_user_queries: Are dried figs good for diabetics?
How many dried figs can I eat per day?
Are dried figs high in potassium?
Dried fig vs fresh fig nutrition
Are dried figs good for blood pressure?
